You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.

The task is to diagnose and fix resume-audit mismatches (illegal moves/visibility) so that running the matrix script produces /app/output/resume-audit.json where all cases are ok, illegal counts are 0, signatures reconcile as specified, and the JSON matches the records JSONL and log rows. First step is to run the matrix to reproduce the issue, inspect the audit output, then narrow to failing cases using one-case runs, and finally inspect relevant maps/scripts/config and engine code to fix resume path divergence.
Plan: 1) Run the full matrix to generate resume-audit.json. 2) Summarize which cases fail and why by inspecting ok/illegal/sig and any log hints. 3) For failing cases, run one-case to reproduce in isolation and compare fresh vs resume move lists. 4) Validate that records JSONL and log match the JSON for a failing case to understand whether the problem is in generation or serialization. After we identify the first concrete failing discrepancy, we can inspect the engine code path for resume vs fresh (likely state/visibility/blocked-tile handling) and patch; but first we need the failing details.

Visibility differs too (fresh_seen 9 vs resume_seen 8), suggesting resume path is missing a visible tile / line-of-sight update. Sentinel additionally has an active 'ward' effect (epoch 7), which may be related to visibility/state rebuild on resume. Next we need to diff the move lists between fresh and resume for these cases, and verify whether JSON/records/log disagree or if the engine produced different resolved moves. Then isolate with one-case runs and inspect their workspace trace/records.
Plan: 1) Inspect rook/sentinel case entries in the audit to see fresh/resume moves and any per-move legal flags. 2) Validate that the records.jsonl and trace.tsv for rook and sentinel match the moves listed in the audit (to ensure serialization is consistent). 3) Run one-case for rook and sentinel to reproduce in isolation and compare to the matrix row. 4) If differences persist, inspect the input definitions for rook/sentinel (maps/scripts/config) and then locate engine code handling resume state/visibility/effects.

steps.message:
Analysis: Rook and sentinel failures reproduce in single-case runs. The moves list is identical across both paths (two step moves with different turn numbers), and records/log match the JSON, so the bug is not in serialization. The only reported difference is visibility (fresh_seen 9 vs resume_seen 8) and signatures differ between fresh/resume despite identical resolved moves, implying the signature computation includes visibility (or some other state) when it should only cover the resolved move list, or the move list used for sig differs from what is written into moves/records. Next we should locate where sig is computed and what inputs it hashes, then adjust to hash only the resolved move list. Also investigate why resume_seen differs (likely missing a final visibility recompute on resume). We'll search the code for 'sig' and resume audit generation.
Plan: 1) Ripgrep for signature computation and audit writer code (keywords: sig, signature, audit, resume-audit, records.jsonl, trace.tsv). 2) Inspect the implementation to confirm what data is included in sig and whether it is incorrectly using visibility/effects/turn counters. 3) Patch sig to hash only the resolved moves list (in the same canonical order/fields as written to moves/records). 4) Rerun one-case rook/sentinel and then full matrix to confirm ok=true across all cases.

Signature values in the audit do not match a naive sha256-over-json(moves) guess, so we must locate the actual signature algorithm in the code or in rogctl. Next step: inspect repository tree, search with grep for 'resume-audit', 'records.jsonl', 'trace.tsv', and 'sig'. Also inspect scripts that generate the audit to find where sig is produced (likely via rogctl subcommand). Then we can adjust signature computation to be based solely on resolved move list and canonical encoding, and ensure resume path recomputes visibility identically (or at least doesn't affect sig).
Plan: 1) List /app structure to identify source language and locations. 2) Use find+grep to locate audit generation and signature code references. 3) Inspect /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh and one-case.sh to see how rogctl is invoked. 4) Run rogctl with --help (or strings) to find relevant subcommands/options. Once we find the source, patch and rerun failing cases.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
out="/app/output/resume-audit.json"
work=""
one_case=""
while [ "$#" -gt 0 ]; do
  case "$1" in
    --out) out="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --work) work="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    --case) one_case="$2"; shift 2 ;;
    *) shift ;;
  esac
done
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$out")"
make -s -C /app build
args=("--out" "$out")
if [ -n "$work" ]; then
  args+=("--root" "$work")
fi
if [ -n "$one_case" ]; then
  args+=("--case" "$one_case")
fi
/app/bin/rogctl "${args[@]}"

Analysis: Project is Go-based. The scripts just build then run /app/bin/rogctl which generates the audit. grep shows key code in internal/f5/run.go (records/log paths) and internal/sim/types.go (Sig field). We need to inspect internal/f5/run.go and related sim types to see how fresh/resume summaries and signatures are computed. Likely the signature is derived from something other than the written moves or uses map iteration order or includes visibility. We'll open the relevant Go files and track where PathSummary.Sig is set.
Plan: 1) Open internal/sim/types.go to see struct definitions for MoveRow and PathSummary. 2) Open internal/f5/run.go around where it runs fresh/resume and writes summaries/signatures. 3) Search in internal for assignments to .Sig or functions computing signatures. 4) Once identified, patch signature computation to be deterministic and based only on the resolved move list rows in canonical order, matching what is emitted. Then rerun one-case rook/sentinel and full matrix.

Analysis: RunRow computes freshMoves via runDirect and resumeMoves via runLoaded, then sets PathSummary.Sig using sigFor(freshMoves) and sigFor(resumeMoves). ok requires fresh.Sig==resume.Sig, which currently fails for rook/sentinel even though move rows appear identical in the output. Therefore sigFor is likely nondeterministic (e.g., iterating over a map) or incorporates hidden state not reflected in MoveRow, leading to different hashes between fresh/resume. Next step: locate sigFor implementation and inspect how it computes signatures.
Plan: 1) Search for sigFor definition in internal/f5 (and elsewhere). 2) Inspect it for map iteration / nondeterminism / inclusion of world state. 3) Patch sigFor to deterministically hash only the move list contents in canonical order, e.g., JSON marshal each MoveRow with fixed field order or manual fmt string, then sha256 and truncate. 4) Rebuild and rerun one-case rook and sentinel, then full matrix.

Analysis: sigFor hashes Turn/coords and iterates over m.Actor+m.Kind but crucially it does NOT incorporate m.Legal. In rook/sentinel, the two move rows differ only by Turn (3 vs 2 for rook fresh/resume portions etc.)? Actually freshMoves and resumeMoves likely each contain one row; their turns differ between paths (fresh turn 3, resume turn 2 for rook; fresh 5, resume 4 for sentinel). The combined moves list shows both rows, but sigFor is computed separately per path, so if the turn numbers differ between fresh and resume, signatures will differ even if the spatial action is the same. That indicates runLoaded is producing moves at a different turn than runDirect, likely because w.Turn handling around save/resume is off by one or uses blob.Turn vs step.Turn incorrectly. Also visibility count differs, consistent with turn misalignment. We need to make turn numbering consistent between direct and loaded runs: after loading blob, w.Turn is set to item.Turn, then in runLoaded it sets w.Turn = step.Turn when encountering 'save' steps and applies lit/advanceAll. In runDirect, it skips 'save' and then sets w.Turn=step.Turn for subsequent steps. If script contains a hostile action at turn 3 in direct path, but in loaded path the hostile move happens at turn 2, suggests applyWorld blob.Turn might be 2 and the subsequent step.Turn might be 2 when processing save, but direct uses step after save with turn 3. Likely runLoaded should start processing steps AFTER save (like runDirect), not at the save step itself. Currently it only processes steps where step.Action == 'save', which seems wrong: it advances hostiles immediately at save boundary rather than on hostile step after save. Fix: in runLoaded, mimic runDirect pastSave logic but starting from loaded state at save; iterate steps after save, applying player/hostile actions similarly, using merged obs for hostile visibility cache maybe. That should align turn numbers and visibility.
Plan: 1) Inspect script steps for rook and sentinel to confirm turn sequence around save and hostile action. 2) Patch runLoaded to replay steps after save instead of triggering on save steps only. It should: applyWorld(blob), prepare merged obs, sync effects, then iterate steps with pastSave=true after encountering save, same as runDirect; when actor P, applyPlayer+ApplyLit; when actor H, advanceAll using merged obs? likely buildObs(w, merged)?? Actually merged is obs map; advanceAll expects cache map[string]bool; we can pass merged. But merged currently includes observed hostiles visibility at save; for subsequent turns we may need to recompute obs when player moves; but resume path is about using cached obs until updated? For now, mimic direct by using buildObs(w,nil) each hostile step; but incorporate merged/stale only immediately after load. We'll set an obsCache variable that starts as merged and gets refreshed on player actions by buildObs(w,nil). 3) Rebuild and rerun one-case rook/sentinel and matrix.

Previously runLoaded applied e4.ApplyLit at save boundary; we removed that, so now lit isn't applied before first hostile step. Fix: ensure e4.ApplyLit runs once immediately after applyWorld(blob) (and maybe after syncing effects) to populate Lit/Seen consistently, and initialize obs to buildObs(w,nil) if merged empty. Also resume_seen 0 suggests w.Seen slice length mismatch after applyWorld? but applyWorld copies item.Seen; maybe item.Seen is nil/empty in blob. In packWorld, it stores w.Lit and w.Seen directly (slices) without copying; then a0.PackItem likely compresses; but playUntilSave never calls e4.ApplyLit at save time, so w.Lit/Seen could be all false when saved unless updated on player steps. For rook/sentinel, perhaps no player step before save, so save snapshot has empty Seen/Lit. Fresh path still applies lit before hostile planning (runDirect applies lit on player steps only; but if no player steps after save, still fresh_seen 9 at end due to earlier ApplyLit in playUntilSave? Actually playUntilSave applies lit only on player steps too; hmm). So we should apply lit at the moment of save in playUntilSave, and also right after loading in runLoaded, to ensure consistent visibility baseline.
